steps.title: Set up: comparing data sets F and G
steps.content: Data set F contains 57 integers, all between 160 and 280. Data set G contains all 57 integers from F, plus the integer 12. So G has 58 integers total. Crucially, 12 is far smaller than every value in F.

steps.title: Test the mean: set up the inequality
steps.content:
Let $S$ = sum of the 57 integers in F. Then Mean(F) $= \frac{S}{57}$ and Mean(G) $= \frac{S + 12}{58}$.

For the mean of F to be less than the mean of G:
$$\frac{S}{57} \lt \frac{S + 12}{58}$$
Cross multiply: $58S \lt 57(S + 12)$, which simplifies to $58S \lt 57S + 684$, so $S \lt 684$.

However, every integer in F is at least 160, so $S \geq 57 \times 160 = 9120$. Since $S \geq 9120$, the inequality $S \lt 684$ is false. Therefore, Mean(F) $\gt $ Mean(G), not less.

steps.title: Test the median: position shift when adding the minimum
steps.content:
When F is sorted, the median is the 29th integer (call it $a_{29}$).

When G is sorted, 12 becomes the smallest value, so G looks like: $12, a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{57}$. With 58 integers, the median is the average of the 29th and 30th values. Counting positions: 12 is 1st, $a_1$ is 2nd,..., $a_{28}$ is 29th, $a_{29}$ is 30th.

So Median(G) $= \frac{a_{28} + a_{29}}{2}$.

Since the data is sorted in ascending order, $a_{28} \leq a_{29}$. Therefore: $$\frac{a_{28} + a_{29}}{2} \leq a_{29}$$
which means Median(G) $\leq$ Median(F). The median is NOT less for F.

steps.title: Conclusion: neither statistic must be less
steps.content: Both the mean and the median are greater for F than for G (or equal in the median case). Therefore, neither I nor II must be less for data set F than for data set G. D is correct.
